skill.name: api-dev
skill.description: Guides API design and implementation with async patterns, rate limiting, versioning, OpenAPI documentation, testing, caching, and production checklists.
skill.summary: Build modern, production-ready APIs with reusable architecture and testing patterns.
skill.author: Azeem-2

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution findings are false positives from Markdown code fences, and credential-access findings are Redis URL examples rather than environment reads. The skill is documentation-only, but it includes an unsafe pickle deserialization cache pattern that should be warned about before publication.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe Pickle Deserialization in Cache Example
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The performance cache example deserializes Redis data with pickle.loads and stores objects with pickle.dumps. If copied into production and the cache is poisoned or attacker-controlled, this can lead to arbitrary code execution. The issue is unsafe guidance, not active executable behavior in the skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code imports pickle and directly calls pickle.loads on Redis data. The threat is clear if the example is copied, although the skill itself is Markdown documentation.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Backticks
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The external command findings point to triple backtick Markdown fences around Python and YAML examples. No shell command execution, Ruby backtick execution, or subprocess invocation is present at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown delimiters for fenced code blocks. There is no command execution syntax or executable wrapper around them.

low_findings.title: Network Calls Are API Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious behavior. The network findings are HTTP client imports, example client calls, example server URLs, and test client configuration. They match the skill purpose and do not show credential exfiltration or hidden outbound requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is presented as API implementation and testing examples. Network usage is expected for this domain, and no secret transfer target is shown.

low_findings.title: Redis URL Parameters Misclassified as Credential Access
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged credential-access lines define redis_url parameters and pass them to Redis clients. No process environment access, hardcoded database secret, or credential extraction pattern was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged values are constructor parameters and Redis connection setup. I found no environment-variable reads or concrete credentials in those snippets.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Matches Are Documentation Text
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak-crypto findings point to prose, field descriptions, OpenAPI metadata, and pagination schema text. No MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar weak cryptographic implementation was found at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines contain natural-language descriptions and OpenAPI example fields. I found no weak cryptographic algorithm use at these locations.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Matches Are Test Data Access
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The system-reconnaissance findings are user_id assignments and assertions inside API tests. They do not query host users, system identity, or machine metadata.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code reads user identifiers from API responses in test cases. There is no operating-system reconnaissance behavior.

low_findings.title: Compound Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious intent. The critical heuristics combine Markdown fences, API examples, and Redis URL examples. No credential exfiltration, evasion, or runnable command chain was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious categories do not combine into malicious behavior after semantic review. The only confirmed issue is unsafe cache deserialization guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pickle Used for Cache Serialization
dangerous_patterns.description: The cache pattern serializes arbitrary values with pickle and deserializes cached bytes with pickle.loads. Safer API guidance should use JSON, MessagePack, or another constrained serializer for untrusted or shared cache data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct pickle deserialization is present in the cache example. The risk is reduced only because this is documentation, not installed runtime code.

content.value_statement: API teams need consistent patterns for performance, resilience, testing, and documentation. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code reusable guidance for modern REST and GraphQL service design.
actual_capabilities: Explains async client patterns, connection pooling, and request metrics.
Provides circuit breaker and rate limiting patterns for resilient services.
Shows API versioning approaches using headers, paths, and query parameters.
Defines OpenAPI response schemas, pagination models, and documentation settings.
Outlines async API test cases, integration flows, and production readiness checks.
limitations: Contains reference examples, not a complete runnable API framework.
Requires adaptation for each language, framework, and deployment environment.
Does not replace security review of authentication, authorization, or data handling.
Includes a pickle cache example that should be replaced before production use.

use_cases.title: Standardize Service Architecture
use_cases.description: Use the patterns to align new API services around async execution, middleware, versioning, and consistent response shapes.

use_cases.title: Improve API Reliability
use_cases.description: Apply rate limiting, circuit breakers, caching, and health checks to reduce failures under load.

use_cases.title: Build Test and Documentation Plans
use_cases.description: Create test coverage, OpenAPI schemas, pagination contracts, and production checklists for release readiness.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic API
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the api-dev skill to design a small REST API for users and projects. Include endpoints, response shapes, errors, and basic tests.

prompt_templates.title: Add Resilience Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the api-dev skill to add rate limiting, circuit breakers, retries, and health checks to an existing API design.

prompt_templates.title: Create OpenAPI Guidance
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the api-dev skill to define OpenAPI documentation patterns for pagination, validation errors, authentication errors, and versioned endpoints.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the api-dev skill to review my API architecture for performance, testing, monitoring, versioning, and deployment risks.

output_examples.input: Design a customer API with pagination and validation errors.
output_examples.output: A structured API plan with resource endpoints, pagination behavior, standard error responses, OpenAPI documentation notes, and focused test cases.

output_examples.input: Improve reliability for a slow partner API integration.
output_examples.output: Recommendations covering async client reuse, timeouts, circuit breaker thresholds, rate limits, metrics, and fallback behavior.

output_examples.input: Prepare an API for production review.
output_examples.output: A release checklist for authentication, authorization, caching, compression, monitoring, health checks, documentation, and test coverage.

best_practices: Adapt each pattern to your framework before implementation.
Replace unsafe serialization examples with constrained formats for shared caches.
Pair generated API guidance with tests, threat modeling, and code review.
anti_patterns: Do not copy reference snippets into production without security review.
Do not use pickle for data that might come from a shared or untrusted cache.
Do not treat example URLs, limits, or schemas as final production settings.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a complete API project?
faq.answer: No. It provides reusable patterns and checklists that you adapt to your chosen stack.

faq.question: Can it help with both REST and GraphQL APIs?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance focuses on API design concepts that apply across REST, GraphQL, and service endpoints.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is the included cache example production safe?
faq.answer: No. Replace pickle serialization with a safer format before using cache patterns in production.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: It covers authentication, authorization, validation, rate limiting, and headers at a checklist level.

faq.question: Should teams still perform code review?
faq.answer: Yes. Generated API designs and adapted snippets need normal testing, security review, and operational validation.
